caption: Gross pay and net pay are two different numbers, and only one of them is real money. $600 gross at $15/hr becomes less once federal tax, FICA, and state tax take their share — before you ever see it. Budget off your actual paycheck, not the number on the offer letter.

The excuse is always 'I'll start when I have more.' Compound interest doesn't care how much you start with — it cares how long your money's been working. $10 a week starting today outperforms $50 a week starting five years from now. Time in the market is the actual advantage.

Dog walking: $15-20/walk. Lawn care: $30-50/yard. Tutoring: $20-40/hr. Reselling: 70-90% of listed price after fees. These aren't hypothetical — they're what people are actually earning right now. Pick one, track every dollar, and let it fund your first real savings account.

Income isn't what separates people who build wealth from people who don't — habits are. Track every dollar for a month. Save the first 10% the moment you're paid, not whatever's left over. Know your net worth number even when it's small. These habits compound the same way money does.

Real example: $800/month from a part-time job. $280 covers required expenses, $320 covers everyday spending, and $200 goes straight to savings before it can be spent on anything else. The exact numbers don't matter — the order does. Pay your needs, cap your wants, save first.

You don't need to pick 10 stocks to start investing seriously. The setup professionals recommend most for beginners is one low-cost target-date or total-market index fund — instant diversification, minimal fees. $50 a month clears the bar. The 2026 contribution limit is $7,000/year, so there's plenty of room to grow.
